caveats: a: The occupation source spans much larger finfish and shellfish industries and is not six-digit wage-weighted.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts estimate is based on a small residual population and mismatched vintages, so it may be unstable.
rho: NOAA electronic-monitoring programs largely cover finfish and shellfish fisheries, not the residual code.
rho: AI may reduce regulator or service-provider review costs without reducing vessel labor.
e: Zero is a margin-bridged dataset estimate rather than proof that no potentially eligible operator exists.
e: Rare-species operations may be classified inconsistently under finfish, shellfish, hunting, aquaculture, or other marine fishing.
s5: Permit and vessel transfers may exclude crews, customer relationships, and operating companies.
s5: The frozen zero firm count prevents a meaningful conditional transfer rate without new population evidence.
q: No public contract sample isolates pass-through for the residual species basket.
q: Value capture differs by species, quota ownership, buyer concentration, crew-share method, and whether savings improve cost or allowed catch.
d5: National commercial-fishing landings are dominated by finfish and shellfish outside the code.
d5: The residual category can be moved by a few species or regulatory decisions and lacks a direct five-year forecast.
o: Operator responsibility may consolidate across vessels or shift to vertically integrated buyers.
o: Aquaculture and import substitution affect demand in d5 rather than operator requirement and are not double-counted here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Trip- and season-based repeat revenue from landed catch sold by weight, grade, species, auction, dealer settlement, processor contract, or specialty order, constrained by permits, seasons, areas, quotas, protected-species rules, weather, stock availability, and vessel economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Finfish and shellfish fishing, aquaculture, recreational charters, seafood processing without harvest, dealers and wholesalers without vessels, captive internal harvest, subsistence activity, passive permit or vessel shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market commercial operators catching or taking marine animals other than finfish and shellfish from natural habitat for recurring external sale to dealers, processors, wholesalers, foodservice, research, bait, or specialty-product buyers, including vessels, crews, permits, catch handling, and reporting.

a.bridge: BLS describes fishing workers across commercial finfish, shellfish, marine vegetation, and hunting rather than a wage-weighted task inventory for 114119. I map trip planning, weather and stock analysis, route and fuel optimization, electronic logbooks, species and size recognition, quota and permit checks, maintenance planning, sales, and administration into exposed wages; the broad source may overstate tasks from larger fisheries while the residual code's small crews make physical deck work dominant.
caveats: The occupation source spans much larger finfish and shellfish industries and is not six-digit wage-weighted.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts estimate is based on a small residual population and mismatched vintages, so it may be unstable.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and trip-time study of eligible residual-species operators, separating captain, deckhand, navigation, search, gear, catch handling, maintenance, reporting, compliance, sales, and administration and documenting current sonar, GPS, VMS, electronic reporting, and monitoring.
a.rationale: Navigation, fish-finding, reporting, compliance, catch documentation, maintenance records, and sales contain digital tasks. Locating and safely taking uncommon marine animals, handling gear, hauling, sorting, measuring, icing, repairs, watchkeeping, and vessel operation in variable conditions remain physical and judgment-intensive.

d5.bridge: NOAA stock and landings reports cover US commercial fisheries broadly, not constant-price demand for marine animals excluding finfish and shellfish. I use stable national seafood activity and favorable 2024 determinations for certain coral and sea-cucumber complexes as weak support for persistence, then set a wide range because rare residual species can face abrupt quota, protected-species, climate, habitat, trade, and market changes; national totals likely overstate stability for this tiny category.
caveats: National commercial-fishing landings are dominated by finfish and shellfish outside the code.
The residual category can be moved by a few species or regulatory decisions and lacks a direct five-year forecast.
d5.change_evidence: NOAA and state landings by residual species, ex-vessel real prices, permits, active vessels, annual catch limits, stock assessments, rebuilding plans, trade data, buyer demand, and climate-range projections mapped explicitly to 114119 species and firms.
d5.rationale: Specialty food, bait, research, cultural, and other marine-product markets can persist, while managed stocks and electronic data support sustainable harvest. Small stock bases, protected status, catch limits, shifting ranges, habitat degradation, extreme weather, trade restrictions, substitute imports, and volatile specialty demand create large downside and upside dispersion.

caveats: Zero is a margin-bridged dataset estimate rather than proof that no potentially eligible operator exists.
Rare-species operations may be classified inconsistently under finfish, shellfish, hunting, aquaculture, or other marine fishing.
e.change_evidence: A vessel- and permit-level registry linked to owners, NAICS, dealer landings, payroll, revenue, normalized EBITDA, ultimate control, and species, followed by verification that operating capability resides in a transferable firm rather than a sole vessel or permit shell.
e.rationale: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the LMM EBITDA band, making a share of that empty estimated population undefined. Public sources do not identify a defensible alternative population of standalone 114119 firms with operating vessels, external recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, and transferable control.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 114119 ($43,758,840) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 114119 ($112,093,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $407,611 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 114119); class <5: 112 firms, avg revenue/firm $371,321 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,854 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,181,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $94,952 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 0

caveats: Operator responsibility may consolidate across vessels or shift to vertically integrated buyers.
Aquaculture and import substitution affect demand in d5 rather than operator requirement and are not double-counted here.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory approval and commercial deployment of autonomous fishing vessels, remotely operated capture gear, processor or government insourcing, permit-holder consolidation, software-only quota models, and evidence that residual products shift entirely to aquaculture or synthetic substitutes.
o.rationale: Catching wild marine animals requires an accountable licensed operator to run a vessel, locate and take catch, handle gear and animals, comply with permits and protected-species rules, maintain safety, and land product. Software and monitoring can support or centralize decisions but cannot deliver the current physical catch basket without field operations.

caveats: No public contract sample isolates pass-through for the residual species basket.
Value capture differs by species, quota ownership, buyer concentration, crew-share method, and whether savings improve cost or allowed catch.
q.change_evidence: Dealer settlements, auction and ex-vessel price records, processor contracts, crew-share agreements, quota and permit terms, fuel surcharges, buyer concentration, trip costs, and margins before and after electronic workflow or navigation improvements.
q.rationale: Fuel, search time, trip planning, reporting, and downtime savings can be retained within a season when dockside prices are market-driven or catch is pre-sold. Quotas and seasons cap volume, dealers and processors may have bargaining power, auction prices are transparent, competitors adopt similar tools, and buyers can reprice contracts, limiting five-year retention.

caveats: NOAA electronic-monitoring programs largely cover finfish and shellfish fisheries, not the residual code.
AI may reduce regulator or service-provider review costs without reducing vessel labor.
rho.change_evidence: Pre/post deployments on comparable small commercial vessels covering electronic logbooks, route and fuel planning, video or image species recognition, catch and discard records, compliance alerts, maintenance, and sales, measuring crew hours, fuel, trip yield, errors, review time, downtime, and violations.
rho.rationale: Electronic reporting, route and weather support, document automation, species-image assistance, and maintenance or sales workflows are implementable within five years. Small crews, harsh environments, unreliable connectivity, limited training data for rare species, hardware cost, regulatory approval, privacy, false identification, and safety-critical judgment constrain realization.

caveats: Permit and vessel transfers may exclude crews, customer relationships, and operating companies.
The frozen zero firm count prevents a meaningful conditional transfer rate without new population evidence.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of Coast Guard vessel ownership, NOAA and state permit and quota transfers, dealer-license changes, brokered vessel sales, and business transactions matched to a verified eligible-firm denominator and screened for completed operating-company control transfers.
s5.rationale: No eligible LMM-firm denominator is established and no source measures qualifying control transfers for this residual industry. Vessel, permit, quota, family, and asset transfers cannot be assumed to constitute sales of transferable operating firms.

principal_driver: Electronic reporting, monitoring, trip planning, navigation, compliance, and maintenance create bounded AI opportunity around labor-intensive vessel operations.
principal_weakness: The frozen LMM count is zero and residual-species demand is too heterogeneous and data-poor to establish transferable-firm opportunity.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as commercial catching or taking of marine animals other than finfish and shellfish and distinguishes aquaculture and the adjacent fishing industries.
sources.title: NAICS 114119 Other Marine Fishing

sources.supports: Lists fish-finding, navigation, repairs, catch sorting and storage, legal-size checks, and manual gear handling; describes hazardous physical work and projects fishing and hunting employment down 5 percent from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: Fishing and Hunting Workers

sources.supports: Describes electronic monitoring with cameras, GPS, transmitters and gear sensors; electronic reporting; vessel monitoring; and AI for automated video processing and prediction of fishing activity.
sources.title: Electronic Technologies

sources.supports: Reports fourteen implemented US electronic-monitoring programs and describes computer-vision development for species identification, weight and length estimation, gear enumeration, and detection of fishing activity, while noting video review, transmission, and storage costs.
sources.title: Electronic Monitoring

sources.supports: Reports 23 stocks subject to overfishing, 42 overfished stocks, and favorable 2024 determinations for Caribbean coral, sea-urchin, and sea-cucumber complexes, illustrating stock-specific regulation and uncertainty.
sources.title: Status of Stocks 2024

sources.supports: Reports total US commercial landings of 8.4 billion pounds valued at $5.1 billion in 2023, used only as broad commercial-fishing context because residual-code landings are not isolated.
sources.title: Fisheries of the United States Reports

title: Other Marine Fishing
